skill.name: swarm
skill.description: Autonomous multi-agent workflow system for complex coding tasks, using a shared plan, review gates, and headless Codex or Claude agents.
skill.summary: Coordinate autonomous coding agents through a shared plan, review gates, and completion checks.
skill.author: nbashaw

security_audit.summary: Most static findings are false positives from Markdown examples, shell snippets, and the .md extension, including the weak cryptography alerts. The confirmed issue is that the bundled runner intentionally starts Codex full-auto or Claude with permission checks skipped, giving autonomous agents broad ability to change local files. No evidence found of malicious exfiltration or embedded prompt-injection text, but the execution model is too permissive for normal publication without strong warnings.

high_findings.title: Autonomous Agents Run With Disabled Permission Controls
high_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run a shell script that starts Codex with full-auto mode or Claude with permission checks skipped. This is expected for the workflow, but it gives spawned agents broad authority to edit files and run commands in the project.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly invokes codex exec --full-auto and claude --dangerously-skip-permissions. The surrounding documentation confirms this is deliberate autonomous execution, not a harmless example.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Plan Content Drives Privileged Agent Work
medium_findings.description: The prompt tells each headless agent to read the plan file, execute incomplete steps, update checkboxes, and create the completion marker. A malicious or poorly reviewed plan can therefore steer agents into unsafe file changes or command execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The execution prompt uses the plan file as shared state and instructs agents to act on it. This is legitimate workflow behavior, but it creates a clear indirect prompt-injection and unsafe-instruction risk.

medium_findings.title: Hidden Completion Marker Can Be Created Prematurely
medium_findings.description: The workflow treats a hidden .context done file as the completion signal. The plan template and runner both instruct agents to create this marker, so completion depends on agent discipline rather than an independently enforced verification result.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The marker path and touch instruction are explicit. The risk is workflow integrity rather than malware, because any agent action that creates the file can end the loop.

medium_findings.title: Broad Repository Writes Are Part Of Normal Operation
medium_findings.description: The plan template encourages agents to run tests, update files, and commit all changes after each phase. This behavior is useful for development automation, but it is unsafe in untrusted repositories or sessions without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The template explicitly allows broad git operations and the skill targets multi-file coding tasks. This is legitimate but expands the blast radius of mistakes or malicious plan instructions.

low_findings.title: Static Markdown And Weak Crypto Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The many Ruby backtick and weak cryptography alerts come from fenced Markdown examples, inline command names, and the .md file extension. I did not find evidence that the skill implements weak cryptography or Ruby shell backtick execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are Markdown metadata or documentation blocks, not executable cryptographic code. The actual executable shell script does not contain weak crypto routines.

low_findings.title: Test Script Uses Temporary Files And Cleanup
low_findings.description: The test script writes a temporary plan under /tmp and removes test completion markers under .context. This is normal test isolation and cleanup, not data exfiltration or destructive behavior beyond its own test artifacts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths are test-scoped and cleanup is limited to the generated test plan and test marker pattern. No evidence found of broad deletion or secret access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Permission-Bypassing Agent Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The runner starts autonomous coding agents using flags that remove normal confirmation barriers.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous flags are present in the executable script and explained in the skill instructions. This directly confirms the pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Prompted File Modification Loop
dangerous_patterns.description: Each iteration asks the agent to execute plan steps, update the plan file, append logs, and create a done marker. This creates an autonomous command loop controlled by plan content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The loop prompt is explicit and repeats until the marker appears or the iteration limit is reached. The risk is inherent to the design.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hidden Done File Controls Termination
dangerous_patterns.description: The script stops when a hidden .context marker exists, and the agent prompt tells Gatekeeper agents to create it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The marker is directly checked by the loop. The concern is reliable workflow completion, not hidden persistence.

content.value_statement: Complex code changes often need planning, repeated verification, and careful handoffs. This skill creates a shared plan and runs autonomous Codex or Claude agents through review gates.
actual_capabilities: Guides the user through goal, context, test strategy, and plan location questions.
Creates a structured plan with phases, checkboxes, agent roles, and a shared log.
Runs a Bash loop that launches Codex by default or Claude when requested.
Supports dry-run and verbose modes for previewing or monitoring the workflow.
Uses a Gatekeeper checklist before agents create the completion marker.
Includes a test script for basic runner behavior and flag handling.
limitations: Requires local Codex CLI or Claude CLI access to run autonomous agents.
Uses full-auto or skipped-permission modes, which can change files without prompts.
Not appropriate for trivial fixes, single-file edits, or tasks needing frequent user input.
Completion depends on agents following the plan and creating the done marker correctly.

use_cases.title: Refactor A Module
use_cases.description: Break a multi-file refactor into phases with tests, review checkpoints, and a final verification pass.

use_cases.title: Add Test Coverage
use_cases.description: Plan and execute a coverage improvement effort while agents log progress and verify results after changes.

use_cases.title: Manage A Migration
use_cases.description: Coordinate a codebase migration with repeated checks, simplification review, and a final merge readiness decision.

prompt_templates.title: Create A Basic Swarm Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a defined coding task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use swarm to create a plan for this task: [describe the goal]. Ask me for missing context before starting.

prompt_templates.title: Run A Refactor Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate use with known files and tests.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use swarm for a refactor of [module or directory]. The success command is [test command]. Put the plan at [plan file].

prompt_templates.title: Add Verification Gates
prompt_templates.prompt: Use swarm to plan [change]. Include reviewer, simplifier, tester, and gatekeeper checks. Require evidence before completion.

prompt_templates.title: Resume And Audit Progress
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced recovery after an interrupted or incomplete run.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use swarm to inspect [plan file], summarize current progress, identify blockers, and continue only if the plan is still safe.

output_examples.input: Use swarm to add integration tests for the billing flow.
output_examples.output: The assistant asks for test commands and key files, then creates a phased plan with coverage goals, review gates, and a Gatekeeper checklist.

output_examples.input: Run swarm for the auth refactor plan.
output_examples.output: The assistant starts the runner in the background, reports the task identifier, and waits for completion before summarizing final checks.
The assistant reads the latest log entries, explains the blocker, and offers to resume, revise the plan, or finish manually.

best_practices: Use the skill only in trusted repositories with version control enabled.
Define exact test, build, and lint commands before starting the runner.
Review the plan and final diff before accepting agent-created changes.
anti_patterns: Do not run the swarm on untrusted plans or copied instructions without review.
Do not use it for simple edits that a single assistant turn can complete.
Do not treat the done marker as proof that the code is correct.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It creates a shared coding plan and runs autonomous agents through implementation, review, testing, and completion checks.

faq.question: Does it work with Claude and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The runner defaults to Codex and includes an option for Claude.

faq.question: Is it safe to run in any project?
faq.answer: No. It uses highly permissive agent modes, so it should only run in trusted repositories with reviewable changes.

faq.question: When should I use it?
faq.answer: Use it for multi-file features, refactors, migrations, and test coverage work that benefits from structured iteration.

faq.question: When should I avoid it?
faq.answer: Avoid it for trivial edits, unclear goals, untrusted instructions, or tasks that need frequent human decisions.

faq.question: How does completion work?
faq.answer: A Gatekeeper agent should verify the plan, log a GO decision, and create a hidden done marker that stops the runner.
